An architecture is being designed to search through candidate ideas. It may need a forgetting mechanism, because without one it risks drowning in accumulated possibilities. But forgetting is dangerous: some breakthroughs come from returning to ideas that once looked wrong or useless, then seeing them differently when constraints change. So the architecture faces a structural tension. If it forgets aggressively, it may lose the very material that later conditions would make valuable. If it forgets weakly, it may preserve too much and fail to discriminate.

A natural response is to add a filter for explanatory quality. That seems attractive because it promises to keep what is not merely possible but illuminating. Yet the premises make this unstable. The point where novelty begins is exactly where explanatory quality is hardest to define. Novel explanations often arrive looking awkward, partial, or poorly motivated. A filter tuned to familiar standards will therefore tend to reject some of the most important candidates. So the architecture cannot safely rely on an explanatory-quality filter to solve the forgetting problem. The two mechanisms interfere: forgetting removes candidates before later reinterpretation, while filtering removes them because they do not yet resemble good explanations.

This makes question selection central. The example “why does light always travel at the same speed regardless of observer” names a kind of question whose fruitfulness may not be obvious in advance. Asking that rather than some neighboring question can reorganize an entire search space. Therefore the architecture’s success depends not only on evaluating answers but on generating questions that change what counts as a good answer. If it cannot originate such questions on its own, then it remains dependent on an external source for the deepest reorganizations of inquiry. If it can, then its question-generation process must be allowed to produce prompts that, at first, may seem oddly framed or insufficiently motivated—exactly the region where explanatory filters are least reliable.

The two nonsensical-looking premises sharpen this collision rather than resolving it. “Strafer tumultuousness skimmingly noncompletion oversimplify” and “Reconciliating ensignhood halophilous woodsman soreheaded” are strings that present themselves without clear explanatory quality, obvious relevance, or stable interpretation. Under a strict filter they would be discarded immediately as noise. Under the warning about novelty, however, immediate discarding cannot be assumed safe, because apparent awkwardness is not decisive evidence of worthlessness. Yet retaining everything that looks like this would paralyze the system.

So the world implied by the premises is one in which a successful architecture cannot equate current legibility with future value. It must somehow preserve enough of the awkward, the under-motivated, and even the presently unintelligible to permit later recovery, while still limiting overload. Its hardest problem is not merely remembering or forgetting, but deciding what may deserve to survive before there are good reasons for its survival.
